stories.title: The Amber Thief's Gambit
stories.text:
Marin the Copper-Handed had three days to steal the Tear of Ashglass before the Seer's Convention dissolved and every temple-guard in Valdris returned to their shrines.

The artifact hung in the Sovereign's Archive, a moonlit drop of crystallized sorrow no larger than a thumb. It could grant visions—true visions, not the fragmentary nonsense the seers usually trafficked in. The Hermitage of Keth'morian had commissioned him, offering a fortune in amber and silence.

Marin scaled the Archive's eastern wall at dusk, his fingers finding purchase in mortar worn by centuries. The lock on the third level was new—Imperial make, clever. He laughed. Locks were language, and he spoke five dialects fluently.

Inside, surrounded by ledgers and starlight, the Tear rested beneath glass. Marin's shadow preceded him, stretched thin and eager. He lifted the case. No alarm. The Seer's Convention had its eyes elsewhere—three of the head magistrates were arguing theology in the great hall below.

But as his fingers closed on the Tear, he saw it: a name inscribed in silver inside the case. His name. Marin. Not a curse—a recognition. Someone had known he would come.

He pocketed the gem and fled into Valdris's midnight streets, laughing like a man half-mad. In the Archive below, a ledger updated itself in invisible ink: *Marin of the Copper Hands. Thief. Employed.*

They had been waiting for him all along.

stories.title: The Singing Cartographer
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer returns to the Ancient Tombs with a new expedition, but the stones have changed. They sing now—not in voices, but in harmonics that shatter bone and reshape flesh. His team fractures: some flee into the Bone Wastes; others are drawn deeper, compelled by the ruins' song to map what should never be charted. Mardoc realizes the ruins are not awakening—they are *remembering* something trapped beneath them. The Star-Rise has cracked the seals the ancients laid down millennia ago. As refugees from the Scorched Reach pour westward, fleeing the worse consequences of the cosmic rewrite, Mardoc must choose: publish his findings and risk triggering mass hysteria, or stay silent and let others blunder into the singing dark unprepared. A fragment of Zareth's stolen memory surfaces in one of the older stones—a vision of the same working, performed a thousand years prior, by the ancients themselves. It ended the same way: catastrophe. Mardoc's hands shake as he records it.

stories.title: The Debt-Collector's Daughter
stories.text: Sevin the Auditor's work tracking Imperial losses from the Star-Rise has made her a target. Merchants whose names vanished from ledgers—converted to living light or stolen wholesale by the cosmic event—now haunt the shadow-markets seeking compensation. A woman approaches Sevin in the archives late at night, claiming to be the daughter of a debt-collector from a village in the Scorched Reach. The village is gone now, erased from maps overnight. The woman says her father's ledgers—records of debts owed across three provinces—have become unstable, the names shifting and changing. Some debtors are appearing in multiple ledgers simultaneously; others have vanished entirely. Sevin realizes the woman is not entirely real—she flickers like a memory struggling to persist. To save her, Sevin must trace the phantom ledger to its source and repay debts in a currency that no longer exists. The adventure takes her to the edges of the reality-rewrite, where the boundary between the mapped world and the unmapped void is dangerously thin.

stories.title: The Warden's Descent
stories.text: Sylas the Deepwarden leads a small team into the fractured Cerulean Trench to investigate reports of an ancient seal breaking. The barrier between the Council of Depths and the Sunken Dark was absolute for ten thousand years; now it weeps. What emerges is not hunger or chaos, but something far worse: *order*. The things from below remember the ancient age and their sealing. They remember the working that trapped them. And now, with the Star-Rise rewriting cosmic mechanics, the seals are no longer binding by the old laws. Sylas must decide whether to retreat and prepare for invasion, or descend further to find the sealed entity's heart and attempt a new binding—one that might hold against a rewritten reality. The abyssal captain accompanying him has already been changed by the touch of abyssal light; her memories are shifting in ways that terrify even her. Time moves strangely in the Trench now.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Vigil
stories.text: Kess of the Sunken Hollows discovers that her curse has metastasized. The magic that forced her to keep the recent dead at rest has expanded: now it compels her to maintain the ancient dead as well—thousands of pre-human corpses whose remains line the caverns beneath the ruins. The singing stones awaken them. Kess must perform a bone-song of such intensity and complexity that it risks burning out her voice entirely, perhaps her mind. If she fails, the pre-human dead will rise and march toward the surface, drawn by the new constellation's light. A young archer from the Shattered Coast, Ashara, arrives at the Hollows seeking shelter from the refugee crisis. She was in the Scorched Reach when the Star-Rise struck and watched her unit dissolve into light. Ashara volunteers to help Kess, but the bone-singer realizes the archer is not entirely present—part of her is caught in the moment of transformation, experiencing it forever in a loop. Together, they attempt the impossible vigil.

stories.title: The Apprentice's Forbidden Archive
stories.text: The Apprentice-Sorcerer, inheritor of the Master's Codex, discovers a sealed manuscript hidden within the Spire of Blackstone: the *Record of Workings*. It documents not one Star-Rise, but seven. Seven times, civilizations attempted to renew the stars. Seven times, they opened voids. Seven times, something vast and ancient stirred. The eighth working—Zareth's—was supposed to be different. But the Codex suggests that the distinction between renewal and summoning is artificial. The sorcerer must decide whether to share this knowledge with Thael the Circle-Keeper and risk destroying the fragile hope that the Star-Rise can be contained, or to remain silent and allow the next working—because there will be a next one—to be performed blindly. Meanwhile, fragments of Zareth's sacrificed memories begin manifesting in the tower itself, appearing as ghostly visions that show the previous workings. Each one is worse than the last.

stories.title: Vex's Phantom Caravan
stories.text: Vex the Memory-Merchant, her memory already fractured by the Star-Rise, discovers that she can no longer find her way home. The roads she has traveled for decades now loop and tangle. In the shadow-markets of Anthrim, she encounters a merchant who looks exactly like her—but claims to be a memory given form. This phantom-Vex is one of the fragments born from Zareth's sacrificed joy and carries Vex's own happiness as cargo. It wants to bargain: if Vex will accept the role of the miserable merchant, the phantom will assume it. Vex refuses, but the phantom follows her caravan as it moves between the fractured regions, slowly poisoning her other merchants, turning them into echoes. Vex must trace the fragment back to its source—the Peak of Aethros, where Zareth performed the working—and either destroy it or find the lost Starborn-Prince and convince him to take back his own joy. The journey traverses the worst-damaged areas of the Scorched Reach, where reality is thin and memories are currency.

stories.title: The Tax Collector's Reckoning
stories.text: The Ledger-Master of a provincial town near the Ash Wastes discovers that taxation has become impossible. The Empire's records are corrupted by the Star-Rise's reality-rewrite; the same merchant can exist in two places simultaneously, creating two tax obligations. Money itself is becoming unstable. Coins minted before the Star-Rise are worth less; some have begun to *age*, crumbling to dust. The Ledger-Master is ordered by Imperial officials to collect a quota from a population that no longer has stable wealth. He discovers that certain refugees carry temporal echoes—they age and de-age unpredictably, their very existence flickering between timeline versions. These echoes can be harnessed as a new form of currency, but only by binding them to official records. The Ledger-Master must navigate the moral collapse of economic order while devising a taxation system based on temporal obligation rather than wealth. Each bound echo costs both the refugee and the collector something irreplaceable: a moment of lived experience.

stories.title: The Wanderer's Fragment
stories.text: A nameless wanderer—neither entirely living nor dead, shaped by the Star-Rise's chaotic edge—moves through the Ashen Wastes collecting stories. She carries a journal filled with names and dates that don't match any calendar, and she is slowly rebuilding something: perhaps a history, perhaps a person. The Hermit of the Barren Wastes encounters her and realizes that the wanderer is made of composite memories—pieces of dozens of people scattered by the Star-Rise, slowly learning to cohere into a single consciousness. The Hermit recognizes fragments of Zareth in the wanderer's eyes. Together, they journey toward the Ancient Tombs, seeking the seal that Zareth's sacrifice cracked. The wanderer's journal begins to stabilize as the ruins draw closer, but stability comes with a terrible price: each recovered memory means the loss of another fragment's perspective. The wanderer must choose which memories to keep and which to sacrifice—a metaphor for identity itself in a reality that is being rewritten.

stories.title: The Refuge at World's Edge
stories.text: At Jade Coast, a haven for refugees from the Scorched Reach and Anthrim, Captain Vesta discovers that the refugees are not entirely present. They remember lives that no longer exist on maps. Towns they fled from have been erased or displaced. Some refugees claim to have fled the same village twice, experiencing two different catastrophes separated by moments or years—they cannot tell which was real. Vesta, a smuggler with a keen eye for hidden cargo, realizes that some refugees are carrying *fragments of the world itself*—pieces of landscape, architecture, memory—that have been displaced by the Star-Rise's rewrite. A black market emerges: traders willing to purchase these fragments, seeking to reconstruct lost places or preserve evidence of what was erased. Vesta must choose between profit and preventing an even greater catastrophe: if the fragments are reassembled, they might open a doorway to the void that the Star-Rise cracked, allowing what was sealed beneath to emerge. The story unfolds through the voices of arriving refugees, each one adding a piece to a puzzle that no one fully understands.
